summary.intent: Add a live Expo web app and local Express+WebSocket API server to the gossamer CLI tool, so users can browse active Claude hook-handler sessions and events from SQLite in real time via a browser.
summary.outcome: All planned files were created and the implementation works end-to-end: the `claude-hook-handler serve` subcommand starts the API server returning real DB data, and the Expo web app bundles cleanly (534 modules) and serves at http://localhost:19006.

repo: Root project uses NodeNext module resolution — all local imports in src/ must use .js extensions.
The gossamer CLI uses Commander.js with a main() function and dynamic imports are used for optional heavy dependencies to avoid loading them on every hook invocation.
Prisma is the ORM for the SQLite DB at ~/.claude/hook-handler.db; db.ts exports the PrismaClient instance.
The app/ subdirectory must be a fully independent npm project with its own tsconfig (extends expo/tsconfig.base) — cannot share the root tsconfig (NodeNext).
WebSocketServer shares port 3000 with Express via { server: httpServer } — no separate WS port needed.

code.finding: Created from scratch. Exports startServer(dbPath, port). Polls db.event.findFirst({ orderBy: { id: 'desc' } }) every 2s and broadcasts { type: 'sessions_updated' } to all OPEN WebSocket clients when max id changes.
Added serve subcommand with --db and --port options after the migrate block. Uses dynamic import('./server.js') so express/ws are not loaded on every hook invocation.
subscribeToUpdates implements exponential backoff WebSocket reconnect: 1s → 2s → ... capped at 30s. Returns an unsubscribe function that closes the WS and cancels the reconnect timer.
react must be 18.3.1 (not 18.3.2 — that version does not exist on npm). react-native must be 0.76.9 for Expo 52 compatibility.

workflow: When an app/ subdirectory has its own package.json inside a monorepo-style project, npm hoists parent node_modules and can resolve react as 'undefined' version, causing peer-dep conflicts. --legacy-peer-deps is the practical workaround.
@expo/metro-runtime must be explicitly installed for Expo web (expo start --web) to work — it is not auto-installed by the expo package.
Dynamic import() in Commander .action() handlers is the correct pattern for optional heavy deps in CLI tools — keeps startup fast for the common hot path.

friction: react@18.3.2 specified in the plan does not exist on npm; required correction to 18.3.1.
react-native@0.76.3 in the plan was outdated; Expo 52 expects 0.76.9.
npm hoisting caused a phantom react@undefined conflict when running npm install inside app/ from a parent directory with its own node_modules.
@expo/metro-runtime was not in the original plan but is required for expo start --web — caused a silent failure until installed.
Port 3000 was already in use during the smoke-test, requiring a workaround to test on port 3334.
open_items: No persistent/daemonized API server setup — users must manually run claude-hook-handler serve in a separate terminal before opening the web app.
The app shows a 'Cannot reach API server' error state on load until the serve command is running; no auto-retry or startup guidance beyond a static text hint.
WebSocket live-refresh path was only code-verified, not tested end-to-end in the browser during this session.
No tests written for the new server.ts HTTP routes or WebSocket polling logic.
No production build configuration for the Expo app (web export/static hosting).
